content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
\ FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Mark W. Steres 
City Attorney 
City of San Dimas 
2361 Rosecrans Ave., Suite 475 
EI Segundo, CA 90245 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-170 
Dear Mr. Steres: 
August 18, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of San Dimas City 
Councilmembers Ryan Vienna and John Ebiner regarding their duties under the conflict of interest 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or 
Section 1090. 
QUESTIONS 
1. Does Councilmember Ryan Vienna have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the 
Act that precludes him from any participation in the City Council's discussions, deliberations and 
actions in regards to the following: 
a. Whether the City wants a Station and Parking Structure (the "Project") within the City; 
b. Comments regarding the design of the Gold Line Station platform; 
c. Comments regarding the design and location of the Gold Line Station Parking 
Structure; or 
d. Comments regarding the design of and the relocation/rearrangement of the City Yard. 
2. If Councilmember Vienna resigned as President of the San Dimas Village Walk Home 
Owners Association (the "HOA"), would that change any of the conflict bf interest analyses and 
conclusions? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-17-170 
Page No. 2 
3. Does Councilmember John Ebiner have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act 
that precludes him from any participation in the City Council's discussions, deliberations and 
actions in regards to the Bonita A venue/Cataract A venue grade crossing located within 500 feet of 
his rental property? 
4. Does the "public generally" exception apply to any of the situations? 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. Based on Councilmember Vienna's ownership interest in his property, the scope of the 
proposed change in use, and the short distance between his property and the Project sites, he would 
have a conflict of interest in all decisions related to the Gold Line Project, including decisions 
regarding whether the City wants a Station and Parking Structure within the City, design of the 
Gold Line Station Platform, the location of the Gold Line Station Parking Structure, and the design 
of and the relocation/rearrangement of the City Yard. Once the City's position on the Station and 
Parking Structure have been approved with finality and without his participation, some future 
decisions may be segmentable as discussed below. You should contact us for further advice once 
the decision on the Project has been finalized. 
2. We did not analyze the relationship of Councilmember Vienna's position on the HOA to 
a conflict of interest since he had a conflict of interest based on his real property. 
3. Councilmember Ebiner will not have a conflict of interest in the decisions concerning the 
railway crossing at Bonita and Cataract Avenues since the decision will not materially affect his 
interest in real property. 
4. The "public generally" exception only applies where 25 percent of the property owners in 
the official's jurisdiction will be impacted by the decision and the official is not uniquely affected. 
You have provided no facts to suggest that the decision will affect 25 percent of the property 
owners in a manner that is similar to Councilmember Vienna. 
FACTS 
The Metro Gold Line Foothill Extension Construction Authority (the "Authority") is a 
public entity created by the California State Legislature pursuant to Section 132400 of the Public 
Utilities Code for the exclusive purpose of completing the design and construction of the Metro 
Gold Line Light Rail System from Union Station in the City of Los Angeles to the City of 
Montclair. 
I Phase I of the extension is an approximately 13.7-mileiline from Union Station to Sierra 
Madre Villa Station in the City of Pasadena. Phase I has been in operation since 2003. Phase 2 of 
the extension is an approximately 24-mile line that continues the extension of the line to the City of 
Montclair. Phase II will be constructed in two phases: 


File No. A-17-170 
Page No. 3 
• Phase 2A from the City of Pasadena to the City of Azusa and 2B from the City of Azusa 
to the City of Montclair. Phase 2A was completed in, and has been in operation since, 
2015. 
• In Phase 2B, the Gold Line will extend into and through the City of San Dimas. 
Pertinent to your request is Phase 2B which is still in design but will have limited 
construction scheduled to begin in the City of Azusa in October 2017. 
Phase 2B. Although the Authority makes all final decisions and is fully in charge of the 
design and construction of Phase 2B of Phase II of the Gold Line, in 2015 the Authority and the 
City of San Dimas entered into a Cooperative Agreement for the Metro Gold Line Phase II/Phase 
2B (the "Agreement"). The Agreement sets forth the role and responsibilities of the City to 
cooperate with the Authority in reviewing and commenting on design plans within the City's 
boundaries, and in reviewing and issuing permits for construction and inspection of construction 
within the City's boundaries. Much of the City's responsibilities pursuant to the Agreement fall 
upon the City staff and not the City Council. The City Council, however, is involved with the 
review of two significant matters related to the design and construction of Phase 2B of the Gold 
Line within the City of San Dimas. 
The two significant matters are as follows: 
1. The design of the grade crossing at the intersection of Bonita Avenue and Cataract Avenue. 
• Bonita A venue is a major arterial street in San Dimas. It currently has railroad tracks 
traversing this intersection diagonally. 
• You stated that because of the configuration of the intersection of Bonita and Cataract and 
the diagonal way the existing railroad tracks traverse this intersection, it is difficult to design 
this intersection grade crossing, especially considering that Gold Line operations will be 
more frequent than current train operations at this intersection. 
• . On August I 0, 2017, you provided the following additjonal facts: Another set of tracks will 
be added alongside the existing tracks (used by the freight trains) and will be used by Metro 
commuter trains (short, four-car commuter trains). 
• While commuter trains are much quieter than current freight trains in use, the frequency of 
commuter-train use will be much greater than freight-train use. Freights are about two trains 
per day at present. Light rail will norma11y operate at I 0- to 15-minute intervals. There is no 
current station/stop at the proposed Gold Line Station location. 
• The California Public Utilities Commission1(CPUC) and the Authority will determine 
whether an above-grade crossing will need to be constructed for the Gold Line operations at 
this intersection. The City Council will review, discuss and provide the Authority with 
comments on the City Council's opinion of whether the grade crossing at this intersection 
should be at-grade or above-grade. The final decision, however, lies within the CPUC and 


File No. A-17-170 
Page No. 4 
the Authority. The City Council will also review, discuss and provide the Authority with 
comments on the design of either an at-grade or above-grade crossing. 
2. The proposed Gold Line Station and Parking Structure located in the City of San Dimas. 
• The Authority has proposed a location within the City for a Gold Line Station and Parking 
Structure that will also require the relocation/rearrangement of a City equipment and 
maintenance yard. 2 
• On August 10, 2017, you provided the following additional facts. The location of the 
proposed Gold Line Station and Parking Structure is currently used as an auto repair shop 
and propane sales business, a portion of an existing kennel, and a portion of the City 
Maintenance Yard. These uses consist of single story tilt-up buildings and surface parking 
lots. The portion of the City Yard that may be used for the proposed Station and Parking 
Structure is not final. But if used, after the Parking Structure is finished there will still be the 
City Yard (reconfigured) and business park/industrial uses will remain adjacent to the new 
parking structure. The parking structure will be used by Gold Line commuters who will 
enter from Arrow Highway. 
• According to the Foothill Gold Line website (http://www.foothillgoldline.org/cities­
stations/san-dimas/), the Station includes the associated 450-space Parking Structure. 
The City Council will be discussing and deciding whether the City wants a Gold Line 
Station and Parking Structure within the City at all. Then, after the Council reviews further 
information and design analyses from the Authority, the Council will consider the Station and 
Parking Structure. 
On August I 0, 2017, you also noted that no other changes to the surrounding area are 
anticipated. Traffic changes will be associated with increased commuter-train trips affecting 
existing roadway operations. However, you stated that the Authority prepared and certified an 
Environmental Impact Report ("EIR") in 2010. The EIR concluded that with mitigation measures 
the traffic impacts at the intersections crossed by th_e new train service will be less than significant. 
Councilmember Vienna was newly elected to the San Dimas City Council in March 2017. 
• Councilmember Vienna owns and resides in a residential condominium located in a 67-unit 
condominium complex known as the "San Dimas Village Walk." The San Dimas Village 
Walk complex is south of and abuts the location proposed for the San Dimas Gold Line 
Station Platform. The proposed location of the Gold Line Station Parking Structure is just to 
the east of San Dimas Village Walk complex. The condominium complex and 
Councilmember Vienna's condominium are within 500 feet of the proposed Gold Line ' 
Station Platform and Parking Structure. 
> Prior to Councilmember Vienna's election to the City Council and, thus, without Councilmember Vienna's 
participation, the City Council reviewed and concurred with the Authority as to the proposed location of the Gold Line 
Station, were the City Council to ultimalely approve the siting of a Gold Line Station within the City. 


File No. A-17-170 
Page No. 5 
• San Dimas A venue is the street from which Councilmember Vienna enters into and exits out 
of his condominium complex. The proposed Parking Structure will only have vehicular 
access at Arrow Highway and not at San Dimas A venue or Commercial Street. 
• From the Parking Structure, there will be pedestrian access to the proposed Gold Line 
Platform. Pedestrian access is also planned from San Dimas A venue to the Platform, and 
from Walnut Avenue to the Platform. There is an existing "Park and Ride" on the west side 
of San Dimas A venue just south of where the rail tracks cross San Dimas A venue. The Park 
and Ride is not for the Gold Line Station, but patrons may park there and walk across San 
Dimas A venue to use the pedestrian access to the Gold Line Platform. 
• There is currently train use on the existing tracks. Councilmember Vienna currently hears 
noise from trains. Councilmember Vienna will have increased train noise upon completion 
and operation of the Gold Line Phase 2B, whether or not the City has a station. It is 
unknown whether the train noise will be greater with a station at the proposed location of 
the Platform. 
• There will be increased noise from patrons at the Platform and from vehicles and patrons in 
the Parking Structure, but it is unknown whether it will be heard in Councilmember 
Vienna's condominium unit. Councilmember Vienna's condominium is three stories tall and 
the surrounding condominium buildings to the north, south and east are also three stories 
tall. You stated that it does not look like he will have views from his condominium unit of 
the Gold Line Platform and Parking Structure, if they are built in the proposed location. 
• Councilmember Vienna is also President of his condominium complex's HOA. The 
condominium complex is within 500 feet of the proposed Gold Line Station Platform and 
Parking Structure. 
Cozmcilmember John Ebiner has been a San Dimas City Councilmember since 1990. 
• Councilmember Ebiner owns a single-family residence that he rents to a tenant/source of 
income. This rental home is on a.residential street but within 500 feet of the intersection of 
Bonita A venue and Cataract A venue. 
• Upon completion of Phase 2B, Gold Line trains will be going through the Bonita/Cataract 
intersection either at-grade or above-grade. Traffic delays at the Bonita/Cataract intersection 
will be less severe if the crossing is above-grade rather than at-grade. Because there is 
currently no design for the potential above-grade crossing, it is unknown whether an above­
grade crossing would affect the view from the backyard of Councilmember Ebiner's rental 
home or increase the train-noise at the rental home. Councilmember Ebiner states that he 
does not believe that the decision nor the design of the intersection crossing ( either at-grade 
or above-grade) will cause any change in the income producing potential of the rental home. 
The City of San Dimas's population is approximately 33,000. The City covers 
approximately 15 square miles. And there are approximately 12,000 residential parcels in the City. 


ANALYSIS 
File No. A-17-170 
Page No. 6 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. 3 A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the 
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of 
his or her immediate family, or on any interest enumerated in Section 87103. The interests pertinent 
to your questions are: 
• Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
• An interest in any source of income, including promised income, aggregating $500 or more 
within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103( c ). ) This would apply to 
Councilmember Ebiner's tenant if the Councilmember has received $500 or more in rent 
from the tenant within 12 months of the decision at issue. 
• A business entity in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or 
holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) The would apply to the HOA of 
which Councilmember Vienna is President. 
• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. (Section 87103.) However, Regulation 18702.S(c) provides that if "the 
governmental decision affects . . .  real property in which the official has a financial interest, 
this regulation does not apply and materiality is detennined under Regulation 18702.1 or 
18702.2." Therefore, we do not further analyze the Councilmember's interest in his personal 
finances. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Initially, we note that the ćct's conflict of interest provisions are generally analyzĈd with 
respect to foreseeability and materiality on a decision-by-decision basis. However, this is not the 
case where decisions are "inextricably interrelated." Decisions are inextricably interrelated when 
the result of one decision will effectively detennine, affinn, nullify, or alter the result of another 
decision. (Regulation 18706(b).) Thus, while you have asked about several decisions, we do not 
separately analyze each decision.-1 
3 Note that an official with a conflict of interest is also prohibited from contacting or appearing before any 
official in any other government agency for the purpose of affecting a decision in which the official has the conflict of 
interest if the official acts or purports to act within his or her authority or on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 
18704(c)(2).) This would apply to recommendations of the City Council to the Authority where the Authority is the 
final decisionmaker. 
4 For example, whether the decision is on the Station or the Parking Structure, these are interrelated decisions. 
Thus, at this time, ifCouncilmember Vienna is disqualified with regard to either decision, he is disqualified as to both. 


File No. A-17-170 
Page No. 7 
In the future, some of decisions may be segmentable. Regulation 18706 provides: 
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a 
financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the 
following conditions apply: 
"(I) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be 
broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the 
decision in which the official has a disqualifying financial interest; 
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented 
from the other decisions; 
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is 
considered first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the 
disqualified official's participation in any way; and 
"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has 
been made, the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a 
reopening of, or otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official 
was disqualified." 
To give rise to a conflict of interest under the Act, the effects of decision on a public 
official's financial interests must be both foreseeable and material. (Section 87103.) Generally, a 
financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable and material if the interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision. Regulation 18701 (a) provides: 
"A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably 
foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a 
governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. A financial 
interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, 
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement 
to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision 
affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 
18702.2(a)(l )-(6)."5 
None of these standards apply to your facts. In all other cases, Regulation 18701 (b) provides 
that if a financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered 
s Regulation l 8702.2(a)( I )-(6) covers the following decisions: "( l) the adoption of or amendment to a general, 
specific plan, and the parcel is located within the proposed boundaries of the plan; (2) the zoning or rezoning of the 
official's property, (3) the imposition, repeal, or modification of taxes, fees, or assessments on the official's property, 
( 4) Authorizing the sale, purchase, or lease of the official's property; (5) the issuance, denial or revocation of a license, 
permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use of or improvement to the official's property, 
(6) construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the official's 
property will receive new or improved services that are distinguishable from improvements and services that are 
provided to or received by other similarly situated properties. 


File No. A-17-170 
Page No. 8 
reasonably foreseeable. However, if the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary 
circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable. 
Regulation 18702.2(a) provides in pertinent part that "[T]he reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect of a governmental decision is material whenever the governmental decision: 
"( 10} Would change the character of the parcel of real property by 
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of 
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise 
levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the 
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial 
interest; 
"( 11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located 
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property, other than 
commercial property containing a business entity where the materiality standards 
are analyzed under Regulation 18702.1. Notwithstanding this prohibition, the 
Commission may provide written advice allowing an official to participate under 
these circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to 
indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the 
official's property; or 
"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and 
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision 
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the 
market value of the official's property." 
Coimcilmember Vienna: The Councilmember owns property within 500 feet of both the 
Station and the Parking Structure. Based on the current use of the property, the addition of the Gold 
Line set of tracks, the Station, and the Parking Structure will significantly increase the intensity of 
use of properties surrounding the Councilmember's property, and affect noise and traffic levels of 
those properties. While these effects may be mitigated in the future, mitigation would not be a part 
of the Authority's approval of the decisions at issue. Based on the size and scope of the decisions 
planned, the effect of those decisions would be material and the Councilmember would have a 
conflict of interest. 6 
In your request, you considered the effect on the Councilmember's view and ambient noise 
levels from his unit. However, Regulations 18702.2(a)(10), (11) and (12) also consider impacts to 
the neighborhood that could affect what a willing buyer would pay to purchase the property (at fair 
market value}. It would not be reasonable to assume that such a significant change in use of 
property within 500 feet of the condominium complex would not influence the value of the 
properties within the complex. 
6 You also noted that Councilmember Vienna is President of his condominium complex's HOA. We have not 
analyzed this aspect of your facts since we have found that the Councilmember has a conflict of interest based on his 
real property interest in his condominium. 


File No. A-17-170 
Page No. 9 
Coimcilmember Ebiner: Councilmember Ebiner owns a single family residential rental that 
is more than 500 feet from the proposed Station and Parking Structure but within 500 feet of the 
intersection of Bonita A venue and Cataract A venue that will be redressed to enable Gold Line 
usage. This means that the impact of the Gold Line project will be less intense on Councilmember 
Ebiner's property. For example, the property will not experience the same effects from the Station 
and Parking Structure that properties within 500 feet of those sites will experience. 
You noted that it is possible that if the new train tracks were at-grade it would cause traffic 
issues at the intersection due to the increased frequency of use. However, since Bonita A venue is a 
major arterial street in San Dimas which may be disrupted by the insertion of the light rail tracks at 
grade, the CPUC and the Authority will consider an above-grade crossing will need to be 
constructed for the Gold Line operations at this intersection. With respect to the intersection 
decisions, the Councilmember's property is not at the intersection or on either of the intersecting 
streets. In fact, the property is separated from the intersection by an undeveloped land 
(approximately the size of a city lot). In addition, there appears to be an alternate route to the south 
that would allow the Council member's tenant to avoid the intersection of Bonita A venue and 
Cataract A venue altogether when accessing the property. 7 
Based on these facts, it is not foreseeable that the market value of the Councilmember's 
property will be materially affected by the intersection-grade decision or the Station or Parking 
Structure decisions. 
As noted above, the Councilmember also has an interest in a source of income/tenant at that 
property. Regulation 18702.3 provides that a source of income will be materially affected if the 
source is a claimant, applicant, respondent, contracting party, or is otherwise named or identified as 
the subject of the proceeding (which is not the case here), or the official knows or has reason to 
know that the source has an interest in a business entity or real property that will be financially 
affected under Regulation t 8702. t (businesses) or t 8702.2 (real property), respectively. 
Regulation 1 8702.2 provides for persons that lease property (such as the Councilmember's 
tenant), the effect is material if the decision would (I) change the termination date of the lease; 
(2) increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property; (3) increase or decrease the rental 
value of the property, and the tenant has a right to sublease the property; (4) change the actual or 
legally allowable use of the real property; or (5) impact the tenant's use and enjoyment of the real 
property. You have provided no facts to suggest that any of these factors would result from any of 
the decisions at issue. 
1 With respect to future noise issues, you stated that the audible bells on the commuter train were much quieter 
and not as obtrusive as current Metrolink horns and that light rail is governed by the CPUC while Metrolink is governed 
by Federal Railway Administration (FRA). The freights that will continue to operate twice a day have louder horns per 
FRA regulations. If the the rail is designed to be above grade,. there would not be any light rail horn at the intersection. 
Also, according to the final 2013 final EIR (http:/lfoothillgoldline.org/images/uploads/3.1 1_-
_Noise_and_ Vibration.pdf), using measures from approximately one block ofCouncilmember Ebiner's property, the 
EIR found that noise would increase by 3..1 decibels (from 60 to 63.1 ). This was considered a moderate impact and 
equates to less than a 5% increase in the noise level. 


Public Generally Exception. 
File No. A-17-170 
Page No. 10 
An official only has a conflict of interest if the decision's effect on the official's interest is 
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103.) Commonly referred to as 
the "public generally" exception, Regulation l 8703(a) sets forth the general rule: 
"A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial 
interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official 
establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his 
or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant 
segment." 
Therefore, in order to invoke the exception, the official must establish that a significant 
segment (25%) of the public is affected and the effect on his or her interest is not unique compared 
to the effect on the significant segment. For purposes of the exception, a "significant segment of the 
public" consists of at least 25 percent of ( 1) the businesses or nonprofit entities; (2) the real 
property, commercial or residential; or (3) the individuals within the official's jurisdiction. 
(Regulation l 8703(b ). ) 
Based on the financial impact on Councilmember Vienna's property caused by the potential 
Gold Line sets of tracks, Station, and Parking Structure, it would appear that 25% of the property 
owners in the City would not be affected in a similar manner to the effect on the Councilmember; 
rather, the effect would be unique to the Councilmember's property and those properties in the 
immediate vicinity of the project. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Jack C. Woodside 
stant General Counsel 
JWW:jgl

sections.question:
1. Does Councilmember Ryan Vienna have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the 
Act that precludes him from any participation in the City Council's discussions, deliberations and 
actions in regards to the following: 
a. Whether the City wants a Station and Parking Structure (the "Project") within the City; 
b. Comments regarding the design of the Gold Line Station platform; 
c. Comments regarding the design and location of the Gold Line Station Parking 
Structure; or 
d. Comments regarding the design of and the relocation/rearrangement of the City Yard. 
2. If Councilmember Vienna resigned as President of the San Dimas Village Walk Home 
Owners Association (the "HOA"), would that change any of the conflict bf interest analyses and 
conclusions? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in3. Does Councilmember John Ebiner have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act 
that precludes him from any participation in the City Council's discussions, deliberations and 
actions in regards to the Bonita A venue/Cataract A venue grade crossing located within 500 feet of 
his rental property? 
4. Does the "public generally" exception apply to any of the situations?
sections.conclusion:
1. Based on Councilmember Vienna's ownership interest in his property, the scope of the 
proposed change in use, and the short distance between his property and the Project sites, he would 
have a conflict of interest in all decisions related to the Gold Line Project, including decisions 
regarding whether the City wants a Station and Parking Structure within the City, design of the 
Gold Line Station Platform, the location of the Gold Line Station Parking Structure, and the design 
of and the relocation/rearrangement of the City Yard. Once the City's position on the Station and 
Parking Structure have been approved with finality and without his participation, some future 
decisions may be segmentable as discussed below. You should contact us for further advice once 
the decision on the Project has been finalized. 
2. We did not analyze the relationship of Councilmember Vienna's position on the HOA to 
a conflict of interest since he had a conflict of interest based on his real property. 
3. Councilmember Ebiner will not have a conflict of interest in the decisions concerning the 
railway crossing at Bonita and Cataract Avenues since the decision will not materially affect his 
interest in real property. 
4. The "public generally" exception only applies where 25 percent of the property owners in 
the official's jurisdiction will be impacted by the decision and the official is not uniquely affected. 
You have provided no facts to suggest that the decision will affect 25 percent of the property 
owners in a manner that is similar to Councilmember Vienna.
sections.facts:

• You stated that because of the configuration of the intersection of Bonita and Cataract and

this intersection grade crossing, especially considering that Gold Line operations will be 
more frequent than current train operations at this intersection. 
• . On August I 0, 2017, you provided the following additjonal facts: Another set of tracks will 
be added alongside the existing tracks (used by the freight trains) and will be used by Metro 
commuter trains (short, four-car commuter trains). 
• While commuter trains are much quieter than current freight trains in use, the frequency of

whether an above-grade crossing will need to be constructed for the Gold Line operations at 
this intersection. The City Council will review, discuss and provide the Authority with 
comments on the City Council's opinion of whether the grade crossing at this intersection 
should be at-grade or above-grade. The final decision, however, lies within the CPUC and  
the Authority. The City Council will also review, discuss and provide the Authority with 
comments on the design of either an at-grade or above-grade crossing. 
2. The proposed Gold Line Station and Parking Structure located in the City of San Dimas. 
• The Authority has proposed a location within the City for a Gold Line Station and Parking 
Structure that will also require the relocation/rearrangement of a City equipment and 
maintenance yard. 2 
• On August 10, 2017, you provided the following additional facts. The location of the 
proposed Gold Line Station and Parking Structure is currently used as an auto repair shop 
and propane sales business, a portion of an existing kennel, and a portion of the City 
Maintenance Yard. These uses consist of single story tilt-up buildings and surface parking 
lots. The portion of the City Yard that may be used for the proposed Station and Parking 
Structure is not final. But if used, after the Parking Structure is finished there will still be the 
City Yard (reconfigured) and business park/industrial uses will remain adjacent to the new 
parking structure. The parking structure will be used by Gold Line commuters who will 
enter from Arrow Highway. 
• According to the Foothill Gold Line website (http://www.foothillgoldline.org/cities­
stations/san-dimas/), the Station includes the associated 450-space Parking Structure. 
The City Council will be discussing and deciding whether the City wants a Gold Line 
Station and Parking Structure within the City at all. Then, after the Council reviews further 
information and design analyses from the Authority, the Council will consider the Station and 
Parking Structure. 
On August I 0, 2017, you also noted that no other changes to the surrounding area are 
anticipated. Traffic changes will be associated with increased commuter-train trips affecting 
existing roadway operations. However, you stated that the Authority prepared and certified an 
Environmental Impact Report ("EIR") in 2010. The EIR concluded that with mitigation measures 
the traffic impacts at the intersections crossed by th_e new train service will be less than significant. 
Councilmember Vienna was newly elected to the San Dimas City Council in March 2017. 
• Councilmember Vienna owns and resides in a residential condominium located in a 67-unit 
condominium complex known as the "San Dimas Village Walk." The San Dimas Village 
Walk complex is south of and abuts the location proposed for the San Dimas Gold Line 
Station Platform. The proposed location of the Gold Line Station Parking Structure is just to 
the east of San Dimas Village Walk complex. The condominium complex and 
Councilmember Vienna's condominium are within 500 feet of the proposed Gold Line ' 
Station Platform and Parking Structure. 
> Prior to Councilmember Vienna's election to the City Council and, thus, without Councilmember Vienna's 
participation, the City Council reviewed and concurred with the Authority as to the proposed location of the Gold Line 
Station, were the City Council to ultimalely approve the siting of a Gold Line Station within the City.  
• San Dimas A venue is the street from which Councilmember Vienna enters into and exits out 
of his condominium complex. The proposed Parking Structure will only have vehicular 
access at Arrow Highway and not at San Dimas A venue or Commercial Street. 
• From the Parking Structure, there will be pedestrian access to the proposed Gold Line 
Platform. Pedestrian access is also planned from San Dimas A venue to the Platform, and 
from Walnut Avenue to the Platform. There is an existing "Park and Ride" on the west side 
of San Dimas A venue just south of where the rail tracks cross San Dimas A venue. The Park 
and Ride is not for the Gold Line Station, but patrons may park there and walk across San 
Dimas A venue to use the pedestrian access to the Gold Line Platform. 
• There is currently train use on the existing tracks. Councilmember Vienna currently hears 
noise from trains. Councilmember Vienna will have increased train noise upon completion 
and operation of the Gold Line Phase 2B, whether or not the City has a station. It is 
unknown whether the train noise will be greater with a station at the proposed location of 
the Platform. 
• There will be increased noise from patrons at the Platform and from vehicles and patrons in 
the Parking Structure, but it is unknown whether it will be heard in Councilmember 
Vienna's condominium unit. Councilmember Vienna's condominium is three stories tall and 
the surrounding condominium buildings to the north, south and east are also three stories 
tall. You stated that it does not look like he will have views from his condominium unit of 
the Gold Line Platform and Parking Structure, if they are built in the proposed location. 
• Councilmember Vienna is also President of his condominium complex's HOA. The 
condominium complex is within 500 feet of the proposed Gold Line Station Platform and 
Parking Structure. 
Cozmcilmember John Ebiner has been a San Dimas City Councilmember since 1990. 
• Councilmember Ebiner owns a single-family residence that he rents to a tenant/source of 
income. This rental home is on a.residential street but within 500 feet of the intersection of 
Bonita A venue and Cataract A venue. 
• Upon completion of Phase 2B, Gold Line trains will be going through the Bonita/Cataract 
intersection either at-grade or above-grade. Traffic delays at the Bonita/Cataract intersection 
will be less severe if the crossing is above-grade rather than at-grade. Because there is 
currently no design for the potential above-grade crossing, it is unknown whether an above­
grade crossing would affect the view from the backyard of Councilmember Ebiner's rental 
home or increase the train-noise at the rental home. Councilmember Ebiner states that he 
does not believe that the decision nor the design of the intersection crossing ( either at-grade 
or above-grade) will cause any change in the income producing potential of the rental home. 
The City of San Dimas's population is approximately 33,000. The City covers 
approximately 15 square miles. And there are approximately 12,000 residential parcels in the City.
sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. 3 A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the 
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of 
his or her immediate family, or on any interest enumerated in Section 87103. The interests pertinent 
to your questions are: 
• Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
• An interest in any source of income, including promised income, aggregating $500 or more 
within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103( c ). ) This would apply to 
Councilmember Ebiner's tenant if the Councilmember has received $500 or more in rent 
from the tenant within 12 months of the decision at issue. 
• A business entity in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or 
holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) The would apply to the HOA of 
which Councilmember Vienna is President. 
• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. (Section 87103.) However, Regulation 18702.S(c) provides that if "the 
governmental decision affects . . .  real property in which the official has a financial interest, 
this regulation does not apply and materiality is detennined under Regulation 18702.1 or 
18702.2." Therefore, we do not further analyze the Councilmember's interest in his personal 
finances. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Initially, we note that the ćct's conflict of interest provisions are generally analyzĈd with 
respect to foreseeability and materiality on a decision-by-decision basis. However, this is not the 
case where decisions are "inextricably interrelated." Decisions are inextricably interrelated when 
the result of one decision will effectively detennine, affinn, nullify, or alter the result of another 
decision. (Regulation 18706(b).) Thus, while you have asked about several decisions, we do not 
separately analyze each decision.-1 
3 Note that an official with a conflict of interest is also prohibited from contacting or appearing before any 
official in any other government agency for the purpose of affecting a decision in which the official has the conflict of 
interest if the official acts or purports to act within his or her authority or on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 
18704(c)(2).) This would apply to recommendations of the City Council to the Authority where the Authority is the 
final decisionmaker. 
4 For example, whether the decision is on the Station or the Parking Structure, these are interrelated decisions. 
Thus, at this time, ifCouncilmember Vienna is disqualified with regard to either decision, he is disqualified as to both.  
In the future, some of decisions may be segmentable. Regulation 18706 provides: 
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a 
financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the 
following conditions apply: 
"(I) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be 
broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the 
decision in which the official has a disqualifying financial interest; 
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented 
from the other decisions; 
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is 
considered first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the 
disqualified official's participation in any way; and 
"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has 
been made, the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a 
reopening of, or otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official 
was disqualified." 
To give rise to a conflict of interest under the Act, the effects of decision on a public 
official's financial interests must be both foreseeable and material. (Section 87103.) Generally, a 
financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable and material if the interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision. Regulation 18701 (a) provides: 
"A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably 
foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a 
governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. A financial 
interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, 
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement 
to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision 
affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 
18702.2(a)(l )-(6)."5 
None of these standards apply to your facts. In all other cases, Regulation 18701 (b) provides 
that if a financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered 
s Regulation l 8702.2(a)( I )-(6) covers the following decisions: "( l) the adoption of or amendment to a general, 
specific plan, and the parcel is located within the proposed boundaries of the plan; (2) the zoning or rezoning of the 
official's property, (3) the imposition, repeal, or modification of taxes, fees, or assessments on the official's property, 
( 4) Authorizing the sale, purchase, or lease of the official's property; (5) the issuance, denial or revocation of a license, 
permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use of or improvement to the official's property, 
(6) construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the official's 
property will receive new or improved services that are distinguishable from improvements and services that are 
provided to or received by other similarly situated properties.  
reasonably foreseeable. However, if the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary 
circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable. 
Regulation 18702.2(a) provides in pertinent part that "[T]he reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect of a governmental decision is material whenever the governmental decision: 
"( 10} Would change the character of the parcel of real property by 
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of 
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise 
levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the 
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial 
interest; 
"( 11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located 
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property, other than 
commercial property containing a business entity where the materiality standards 
are analyzed under Regulation 18702.1. Notwithstanding this prohibition, the 
Commission may provide written advice allowing an official to participate under 
these circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to 
indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the 
official's property; or 
"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and 
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision 
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the 
market value of the official's property." 
Coimcilmember Vienna: The Councilmember owns property within 500 feet of both the 
Station and the Parking Structure. Based on the current use of the property, the addition of the Gold 
Line set of tracks, the Station, and the Parking Structure will significantly increase the intensity of 
use of properties surrounding the Councilmember's property, and affect noise and traffic levels of 
those properties. While these effects may be mitigated in the future, mitigation would not be a part 
of the Authority's approval of the decisions at issue. Based on the size and scope of the decisions 
planned, the effect of those decisions would be material and the Councilmember would have a 
conflict of interest. 6 
In your request, you considered the effect on the Councilmember's view and ambient noise 
levels from his unit. However, Regulations 18702.2(a)(10), (11) and (12) also consider impacts to 
the neighborhood that could affect what a willing buyer would pay to purchase the property (at fair 
market value}. It would not be reasonable to assume that such a significant change in use of 
property within 500 feet of the condominium complex would not influence the value of the 
properties within the complex. 
6 You also noted that Councilmember Vienna is President of his condominium complex's HOA. We have not 
analyzed this aspect of your facts since we have found that the Councilmember has a conflict of interest based on his 
real property interest in his condominium.  
Coimcilmember Ebiner: Councilmember Ebiner owns a single family residential rental that 
is more than 500 feet from the proposed Station and Parking Structure but within 500 feet of the 
intersection of Bonita A venue and Cataract A venue that will be redressed to enable Gold Line 
usage. This means that the impact of the Gold Line project will be less intense on Councilmember 
Ebiner's property. For example, the property will not experience the same effects from the Station 
and Parking Structure that properties within 500 feet of those sites will experience. 
You noted that it is possible that if the new train tracks were at-grade it would cause traffic 
issues at the intersection due to the increased frequency of use. However, since Bonita A venue is a 
major arterial street in San Dimas which may be disrupted by the insertion of the light rail tracks at 
grade, the CPUC and the Authority will consider an above-grade crossing will need to be 
constructed for the Gold Line operations at this intersection. With respect to the intersection 
decisions, the Councilmember's property is not at the intersection or on either of the intersecting 
streets. In fact, the property is separated from the intersection by an undeveloped land 
(approximately the size of a city lot). In addition, there appears to be an alternate route to the south 
that would allow the Council member's tenant to avoid the intersection of Bonita A venue and 
Cataract A venue altogether when accessing the property. 7 
Based on these facts, it is not foreseeable that the market value of the Councilmember's 
property will be materially affected by the intersection-grade decision or the Station or Parking 
Structure decisions. 
As noted above, the Councilmember also has an interest in a source of income/tenant at that 
property. Regulation 18702.3 provides that a source of income will be materially affected if the 
source is a claimant, applicant, respondent, contracting party, or is otherwise named or identified as 
the subject of the proceeding (which is not the case here), or the official knows or has reason to 
know that the source has an interest in a business entity or real property that will be financially 
affected under Regulation t 8702. t (businesses) or t 8702.2 (real property), respectively. 
Regulation 1 8702.2 provides for persons that lease property (such as the Councilmember's 
tenant), the effect is material if the decision would (I) change the termination date of the lease; 
(2) increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property; (3) increase or decrease the rental 
value of the property, and the tenant has a right to sublease the property; (4) change the actual or 
legally allowable use of the real property; or (5) impact the tenant's use and enjoyment of the real 
property. You have provided no facts to suggest that any of these factors would result from any of 
the decisions at issue. 
1 With respect to future noise issues, you stated that the audible bells on the commuter train were much quieter 
and not as obtrusive as current Metrolink horns and that light rail is governed by the CPUC while Metrolink is governed 
by Federal Railway Administration (FRA). The freights that will continue to operate twice a day have louder horns per 
FRA regulations. If the the rail is designed to be above grade,. there would not be any light rail horn at the intersection. 
Also, according to the final 2013 final EIR (http:/lfoothillgoldline.org/images/uploads/3.1 1_-
_Noise_and_ Vibration.pdf), using measures from approximately one block ofCouncilmember Ebiner's property, the 
EIR found that noise would increase by 3..1 decibels (from 60 to 63.1 ). This was considered a moderate impact and 
equates to less than a 5% increase in the noise level. 


Public Generally Exception.  
An official only has a conflict of interest if the decision's effect on the official's interest is 
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103.) Commonly referred to as 
the "public generally" exception, Regulation l 8703(a) sets forth the general rule: 
"A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial 
interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official 
establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his 
or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant 
segment." 
Therefore, in order to invoke the exception, the official must establish that a significant 
segment (25%) of the public is affected and the effect on his or her interest is not unique compared 
to the effect on the significant segment. For purposes of the exception, a "significant segment of the 
public" consists of at least 25 percent of ( 1) the businesses or nonprofit entities; (2) the real 
property, commercial or residential; or (3) the individuals within the official's jurisdiction. 
(Regulation l 8703(b ). ) 
Based on the financial impact on Councilmember Vienna's property caused by the potential 
Gold Line sets of tracks, Station, and Parking Structure, it would appear that 25% of the property 
owners in the City would not be affected in a similar manner to the effect on the Councilmember; 
rather, the effect would be unique to the Councilmember's property and those properties in the 
immediate vicinity of the project.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Does Councilmember Ryan Vienna have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the 
Act that precludes him from any participation in the City Council's discussions, deliberations and 
actions in regards to the following: 
a. Whether the City wants a Station and Parking Structure (the "Project") within the City; 
b. Comments regarding the design of the Gold Line Station platform; 
c. Comments regarding the design and location of the Gold Line Station Parking 
Structure; or 
d. Comments regarding the design of and the relocation/rearrangement of the City Yard. 
2. If Councilmember Vienna resigned as President of the San Dimas Village Walk Home 
Owners Association (the "HOA"), would that change any of the conflict bf interest analyses and 
conclusions? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in3. Does Councilmember John Ebiner have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act 
that precludes him from any participation in the City Council's discussions, deliberations and 
actions in regards to the Bonita A venue/Cataract A venue grade crossing located within 500 feet of 
his rental property? 
4. Does the "public generally" exception apply to any of the situations?

CONCLUSION: 1. Based on Councilmember Vienna's ownership interest in his property, the scope of the 
proposed change in use, and the short distance between his property and the Project sites, he would 
have a conflict of interest in all decisions related to the Gold Line Project, including decisions 
regarding whether the City wants a Station and Parking Structure within the City, design of the 
Gold Line Station Platform, the location of the Gold Line Station Parking Structure, and the design 
of and the relocation/rearrangement of the City Yard. Once the City's position on the Station and 
Parking Structure have been approved with finality and without his participation, some future 
decisions may be segmentable as discussed below. You should contact us for further advice once 
the decision on the Project has been finalized. 
2. We did not analyze the relationship of Councilmember Vienna's position on the HOA to 
a conflict of interest since he had a conflict of interest based on his real property. 
3. Councilmember Ebiner will not have a conflict of interest in the decisions concerning the 
railway crossing at Bonita and Cataract Avenues since the decision will not materially affect his 
interest in real property. 
4. The "public generally" exception only applies where 25 percent of the property owners in 
the official's jurisdiction will be impacted by the decision and the official is not uniquely affected. 
You have provided no facts to suggest that the decision will affect 25 percent of the property 
owners in a manner that is similar to Councilmember Vienna.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA \ FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION · 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Mark W. Steres City Attorney City of San Dimas 2361 Rosecrans Ave., Suite 475 EI Segundo, CA 90245 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-17-170 Dear Mr. Steres: August 18, 2017 This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of San Dimas City Councilmembers Ryan Vienna and John Ebiner regarding their duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. QUESTIONS 1. Does Councilmember Ryan Vienna have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act that precludes him from any participation in the City Council's discussions, deliberations and actions in regards to the following: a. Whether the City wants a Station and Parking Structure (the "Project") within the City; b. Comments regarding the design of the Gold Line Station platform; c. Comments regarding the design and location of the Gold Line Station Parking Structure; or d. Comments regarding the design of and the relocation/rearrangement of the City Yard. 2. If Councilmember Vienna resigned as President of the San Dimas Village Walk Home Owners Association (the "HOA"), would that change any of the conflict bf interest analyses and conclusions? 1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-17-170 Page No. 2 3. Does Councilmember John Ebiner have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act that precludes him from any participation in the City Council's discussions, deliberations and actions in regards to the Bonita A venue/Cataract A venue grade crossing located within 500 feet of his rental property? 4. Does the "public generally" exception apply to any of the situations? CONCLUSIONS 1. Based on Councilmember Vienna's ownership interest in his property, the scope of the proposed change in use, and the short distance between his property and the Project sites, he would have a conflict of interest in all decisions related to the Gold Line Project, including decisions regarding whether the City wants a Station and Parking Structure within the City, design of the Gold Line Station Platform, the location of the Gold Line Station Parking Structure, and the design of and the relocation/rearrangement of the City Yard. Once the City's position on the Station and Parking Structure have been approved with finality and without his participation, some future decisions may be segmentable as discussed below. You should contact us for
